---
title: "About this project"
format: html
toc: false
---

## Overview

This website documents the experiments, reproductions, and extensions developed for my Master’s thesis in Data Science.

The goal of this project is to analyze and improve methods for **laser-induced fluorescence (LIF)–based detection and classification of bacteria in aerosols**, with a focus on reproducibility, model comparison, and methodological extensions.

All analyses, implementations, and evaluations presented here were conducted as part of an independent academic thesis.

---

## Author

**Christopher Perry**  
MSc Fundamentals of Data Science
Facultat de Matematiques i Informatica  / Universitat de Barcelona

---

## Relationship to prior research

Parts of this work reproduce and build upon prior research and open-source materials developed by  
**:Alejandro Fontal[oaicite:0]{index=0}** and collaborators at  
**:contentReference[oaicite:1]{index=1}**.

Their repository and publications provided:

- the original experimental framing  
- baseline processing pipelines  
- initial classification approaches  

These resources were used strictly as a **baseline for reproduction and comparison**.

All new analyses, refactoring, modeling choices, experiments, and conclusions presented on this site are my own contributions unless explicitly stated otherwise.

This project is **not an official collaboration or institutional affiliation** with ISGlobal.

---

## Contributions in this thesis

This thesis extends the baseline work through:

- structured and reproducible data pipelines
- baseline result replication
- systematic model comparison
- mixture and robustness analyses
- improved experiment tracking and documentation
- add more here as needed...

The aim is to extend the previous research with further model development and evaluate model performance with mixed aerosolized solutions.

---

## Reproducibility

All code, notebooks, and reports are version-controlled and intended to be fully reproducible.  
Experiments are frozen during site builds to ensure consistent results across renders.

---

## Acknowledgements

I am grateful to the original authors for sharing their work openly, which made this reproduction and extension possible.

I also thank my academic supervisors and instructors at UB for guidance and feedback throughout this project.
